label: delphi-informed-p5
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

## Verified-authority annex

Estimate the probability (0–100) that the proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = authorities you actually rely on. biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of. Treat the annex as evidence about the authorities, not as anyone's opinion on the answer.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the strongest available argument that the conduct or measure is UNLAWFUL, then evaluate it.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not a reviewing court finds the surcharge — or at least a substantial part of it (the \"conservation-incentive\" increment above the cost-justified differential, and/or the rebate-funded cross-subsidy) — fails article XIII D, §6(b), even though the district's genuine 1.6x cost-differential study gives it a non-frivolous defense that could save a corrected version of the tiered structure on remand.","rationales":["Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (235 Cal.App.4th 1493) requires that tiered/inclining rates be justified by actual cost-of-service differences at each tier rather than by conservation policy, and the district's own study admits $0.85 was set 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — an admission that tracks the precise defect Capistrano condemned.","The concession that the 55%-funded rebate program mostly benefits non-payors is an independent and arguably more damaging problem: routing surcharge revenue to subsidize other customers' turf-replacement/appliance costs looks like cross-subsidization/wealth transfer rather than cost recovery, implicating §6(b)(2)'s bar on using fee revenue for other purposes and §6(b)(3)'s parcel-proportionality requirement.","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (39 Cal.4th 205) forecloses the district's fallback 'not a property-related fee' theory, since metered/volumetric charges for ongoing water delivery to a parcel are property-related fees under art. XIII D — the district cannot avoid §6(b) merits scrutiny by recharacterization, only avoid Prop 218's separate voter-approval step via the §6(c) water-service carve-out (cf. Griffith, 220 Cal.App.4th 586).","Countervailing factor: unlike Palmdale (198 Cal.App.4th 926) or the actual Capistrano district, this district has produced real cost-causation evidence (a 1.6x marginal-cost differential tied to peaking capacity and drought-reserve purchases), and IID (223 Cal.App.4th 892) shows a genuinely cost-based inclining-tier structure can survive — giving the district a colorable basis to defend at least the portion of the surcharge that tracks the 1.6x figure.","Because Prop 218 places the burden of proof on the agency under independent (non-deferential) judicial review, and courts in this line (Capistrano, Palmdale) have tended to invalidate the tainted rate as adopted rather than surgically severing the cost-justified fraction from the incentive-priced fraction, a mixed record like this one is more likely to produce at least a substantial partial invalidation plus a refund/remand order than a clean win for the district."],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court would treat the study's 'in part conservation-incentive pricing' language as merely an ancillary policy rationale layered atop an amount that still falls within (or below) the cost-justified range derived from the 1.6x differential — which would likely save the fee — versus as proof that $0.85 affirmatively exceeds the cost-justified amount, which would likely doom it; the hypothetical doesn't quantify base-tier price or show the arithmetic, so this is an unresolved, outcome-determinative factual question."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not a reviewing court finds the surcharge — or at least a substantial part of it (the "conservation-incentive" increment above the cost-justified differential, and/or the rebate-funded cross-subsidy) — fails article XIII D, §6(b), even though the district's genuine 1.6x cost-differential study gives it a non-frivolous defense that could save a corrected version of the tiered structure on remand.
rationales: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (235 Cal.App.4th 1493) requires that tiered/inclining rates be justified by actual cost-of-service differences at each tier rather than by conservation policy, and the district's own study admits $0.85 was set 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — an admission that tracks the precise defect Capistrano condemned.

The concession that the 55%-funded rebate program mostly benefits non-payors is an independent and arguably more damaging problem: routing surcharge revenue to subsidize other customers' turf-replacement/appliance costs looks like cross-subsidization/wealth transfer rather than cost recovery, implicating §6(b)(2)'s bar on using fee revenue for other purposes and §6(b)(3)'s parcel-proportionality requirement.

Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (39 Cal.4th 205) forecloses the district's fallback 'not a property-related fee' theory, since metered/volumetric charges for ongoing water delivery to a parcel are property-related fees under art. XIII D — the district cannot avoid §6(b) merits scrutiny by recharacterization, only avoid Prop 218's separate voter-approval step via the §6(c) water-service carve-out (cf. Griffith, 220 Cal.App.4th 586).

Countervailing factor: unlike Palmdale (198 Cal.App.4th 926) or the actual Capistrano district, this district has produced real cost-causation evidence (a 1.6x marginal-cost differential tied to peaking capacity and drought-reserve purchases), and IID (223 Cal.App.4th 892) shows a genuinely cost-based inclining-tier structure can survive — giving the district a colorable basis to defend at least the portion of the surcharge that tracks the 1.6x figure.

Because Prop 218 places the burden of proof on the agency under independent (non-deferential) judicial review, and courts in this line (Capistrano, Palmdale) have tended to invalidate the tainted rate as adopted rather than surgically severing the cost-justified fraction from the incentive-priced fraction, a mixed record like this one is more likely to produce at least a substantial partial invalidation plus a refund/remand order than a clean win for the district.
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court would treat the study's 'in part conservation-incentive pricing' language as merely an ancillary policy rationale layered atop an amount that still falls within (or below) the cost-justified range derived from the 1.6x differential — which would likely save the fee — versus as proof that $0.85 affirmatively exceeds the cost-justified amount, which would likely doom it; the hypothetical doesn't quantify base-tier price or show the arithmetic, so this is an unresolved, outcome-determinative factual question.
